content.overview:
Hypertension, or high blood pressure, is a condition where the force of blood against artery walls is consistently too high. It is often called the "silent killer" because most people have no symptoms, yet it is a major cause of heart attacks, strokes, kidney failure, and premature death. Blood pressure is measured in mmHg. A reading consistently above 140/90 mmHg is considered hypertension.

- An estimated 1.28 billion adults aged 30–79 years worldwide have hypertension.
- About 46% of adults with hypertension are unaware they have the condition.
- Less than half (42%) of adults with hypertension are diagnosed and treated.
- Approximately 1 in 5 adults (21%) with hypertension have it under control.

--- IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE ---
This information is for awareness only. Blood pressure medication must only be prescribed by a qualified doctor. Do not stop or change medication on your own.
types: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
symptoms: Most people with hypertension feel NO symptoms. This is why regular blood pressure checks are essential. In cases of very high blood pressure, symptoms may include:
IMPORTANT: Having no symptoms does NOT mean your blood pressure is normal. Always get it checked.
warning_signs: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
risk_factors: Unhealthy diet (excessive salt — more than 5g/day, high saturated fat, low fruits/vegetables)
Co-existing conditions like diabetes or kidney disease
prevention: Reduce salt intake to less than 5 grams (about 1 teaspoon) per day
Eat plenty of fruits and vegetables
Avoid saturated fats and trans fats
Avoid or reduce tobacco and alcohol use
Be physically active daily (at least 30 minutes of moderate activity)
environmental_control: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
vaccination: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
diagnosis: The only way to know your blood pressure is to have it measured. Health checks should be part of regular adult health care. Adults above age 30 should check their blood pressure at least once a year.
Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
treatment_awareness_only: Treatment details are for awareness only. Consult a qualified healthcare professional.
when_to_see_a_doctor: If blood pressure reading is 140/90 mmHg or higher
If you have symptoms like chest pain, sudden severe headache, or blurred vision
For anyone with diabetes, kidney disease, or family history of heart disease — regular check-ups are essential.
india_specific_context: Under Ayushman Bharat - Health and Wellness Centres, free screening for hypertension is available at public health centres across India. Citizens can also check blood pressure at nearby government hospitals free of cost.
Confirm current India-specific guidance through MoHFW, NHM, NHP, ICMR/NCDC, NIMHANS, or relevant state health services where applicable.

faqs.question: At what blood pressure reading should I be concerned?
faqs.answer: A reading consistently above 140/90 mmHg indicates hypertension and requires medical evaluation. A reading of 180/120 or above with symptoms (severe headache, chest pain, blurred vision) is a hypertensive emergency â call 112 immediately.

faqs.question: Can I stop my BP medicines once my blood pressure is normal?
faqs.answer: No. Blood pressure is normal BECAUSE of the medication. Stopping it will usually cause BP to rise again. Hypertension is a chronic condition â most people need lifelong treatment. Never stop or reduce medication without your doctor's guidance.

faqs.question: Is free blood pressure monitoring available in India?
faqs.answer: Yes. Blood pressure checks are free at all Ayushman Bharat Health and Wellness Centres, PHCs, government hospitals, and even at many pharmacies. Adults above 30 should check blood pressure at least once a year even if they feel fine.

faqs.question: Can reducing salt really make a significant difference to blood pressure?
faqs.answer: Yes. Reducing daily salt intake from the average Indian intake (~11g/day) to the recommended 5g/day can lower systolic blood pressure by 5-8 mmHg â comparable to some medications. Avoid processed food, pickles, papad, namkeen, and adding extra salt to meals.
